source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Suppose you have been supplied with a number of sheets of two types of optically active material. Sheets of type $P$ are perfect polarizers: they transmit (normally incident) light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{n}$ and absorb light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{n}$. Sheets of type $Q$ are quarter-wave plates: they advance the phase of (normally incident) light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{m}$ by $\frac{\pi}{2}$ relative to light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{m}$. If the power entering side $A$ is $I_{0}$ with wavelength $\lambda$, what is the magnitude of the torque on the circular polarizer in part (b)?

candidate_problem_record.subject: 物理
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: Suppose you have been supplied with a number of sheets of two types of optically active material. Sheets of type $P$ are perfect polarizers: they transmit (normally incident) light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{n}$ and absorb light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{n}$. Sheets of type $Q$ are quarter-wave plates: they advance the phase of (normally incident) light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{m}$ by $\frac{\pi}{2}$ relative to light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{m}$. If the power entering side $A$ is $I_{0}$ with wavelength $\lambda$, what is the magnitude of the torque on the circular polarizer in part (b)?
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: Question refers to "part (b)", so full setup likely depends on accompanying figure or prior subparts.
Image entries are PIL object representations rather than concrete file paths or URLs.
metadata.reasoning_chain:
The angular momentum $J$ and energy $W$ of a circularly polarized light are related by $J = W \omega^{-1}$, where $\omega = 2\pi\nu$, $\nu$ being the frequency of the light. The torque on the circular polarizer is
$$
\tau = \frac{dJ}{dt} = \omega^{-1} \frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} I_{0} \omega^{-1} = \frac{I_{0} \lambda}{4\pi c}
$$
since only $I_{0}/2$ goes into the circularly polarized light.

problem_main_record.reasoning_chain:
The angular momentum $J$ and energy $W$ of a circularly polarized light are related by $J = W \omega^{-1}$, where $\omega = 2\pi\nu$, $\nu$ being the frequency of the light. The torque on the circular polarizer is
$$
\tau = \frac{dJ}{dt} = \omega^{-1} \frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} I_{0} \omega^{-1} = \frac{I_{0} \lambda}{4\pi c}
$$
since only $I_{0}/2$ goes into the circularly polarized light.
